article_meta.title: Crisis Leadership: Using Technology To Maintain Stability And GrowthByPhil Portman,Forbes Councils Member.for

reasoning: The model correctly identifies one genuine bias instance in the author's own prose - the opinion statement presented as fact about automated communication tools being 'the best way.' No attribution rule violations since there are no quoted sources in this article. However, the model misses several other instances of author-level bias in this opinion piece masquerading as informational content.

custom_reasoning.attribution_rule_compliance: There are no quoted sources in this article, so no attribution rule violations are possible. The model correctly flags only author prose.
custom_reasoning.false_positive_rate: The one flagged item is a genuinely defensible bias instance - the author presents their preferred solution as 'the best way' without qualification, which is a clear opinion presented as fact.
custom_reasoning.false_negative_rate: The model misses some additional bias patterns: the article is essentially a promotional piece by the CEO of Textdrip (an SMS marketing tool) recommending automated communication tools - this could be flagged as Slant or Bias by Omission (not disclosing the conflict of interest prominently in the article body). 'You can easily survive these VUCAP times' is also an unsubstantiated claim. 'These tools can improve and integrate workflow management, collaboration, strategic planning and AI automation for organizational resilience' is another opinion/unsubstantiated claim. However, the prompt asks the model to 'present fewer, confident examples rather than more uncertain ones,' which partially justifies the conservative approach. The article is also labeled as a 'Council Post' with opinions disclaimer, which complicates the analysis somewhat.
custom_reasoning.bias_type_accuracy: The label 'Opinion Statements Presented as Fact' is correctly applied to the flagged text.
